title: "ECON 216 EDA"
author: "Calais Michaelsson, Juliene Jones, Catherina Iltchev"
date: "2026-04-02"

    html_document: 
        toc: true
        toc_depth: 6
        number_sections: true
        toc_float: true
    pdf_document:
        toc: true
        toc_depth: 6
        number_sections: true
        toc_float: true
---
# Setup


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = normalizePath("."))
```
```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(extrafont)
library(tidycensus)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(gtrendsR)
library(haven)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(tibble)
library(ggplot2)
library(forcats)
library(scales)

```
```{r}
library(here)

df <- read.csv(here("okcupid_profiles.csv"))

```
# Essay Questions Data Cleaning: 
### Higher Education Keywords: 
```{r}
high_education_keywords <- c(
  # Direct credentials
  "phd", "ph.d", "doctorate", "doctoral", "dissertation",
  "thesis", "postdoc", "post-doc", "grad school",
  "graduate school", "masters degree", "master's degree",
  "bachelor's degree", "bachelors degree",

  # Academic roles
  "professor", "faculty", "lecturer", "teaching assistant",
  "research assistant", "postdoctoral", "tenure",
  "tenured", "academic", "academia",

  # Research and publication
  "published", "publication", "peer reviewed", "peer-reviewed",
  "journal article", "research paper", "lab", "laboratory",
  "field work", "fieldwork", "grant", "fellowship",

  # Specific degree programs
  "law degree", "jd", "j.d", "md", "m\\.d", "mba", "m.b.a",
  "master of", "bachelor of", "doctor of",
  "phd candidate", "phd student", "doctoral candidate",
  "medical degree", "law degree",

  # Named institutions
  "harvard", "yale", "stanford", "mit", "princeton",
  "columbia", "cornell", "dartmouth", "brown", "upenn",
  "uchicago", "duke", "vanderbilt", "georgetown", "nyu",
  "ucla", "berkeley", "caltech",

  # Professional licensure tied to advanced degrees
  "bar exam", "passed the bar", "board certified",
  "licensed physician", "licensed attorney",
  "residency", "fellowship program", "medical residency"
)

high_edu_pattern <- paste(high_education_keywords, collapse = "|")

# ── Add flag to dataframe ──────────────────

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    high_edu_signal = if_any(starts_with("essay"),
                             ~str_detect(., regex(high_edu_pattern,
                                                  ignore_case = TRUE)))
  )

# ── Keyword frequency table ────────────────

all_essays <- apply(dplyr::select(df, starts_with("essay")),
                    1, paste, collapse = " ")

map_dfr(high_education_keywords, function(kw) {
  tibble(
    keyword = kw,
    count   = sum(str_detect(all_essays,
                             regex(kw, ignore_case = TRUE)),
                  na.rm = TRUE),
    pct     = round(count / nrow(df) * 100, 2)
  )
}) %>%
  arrange(desc(count)) %>%
  filter(count > 0) %>%
  knitr::kable(col.names = c("Keyword", "Count", "% of Profiles"),
               caption = "High Education Credential Keyword Frequency")
```
### Responses containing "m.d"
```{r}
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Search a specific keyword and view
# 50 essay responses containing it
# ─────────────────────────────────────────

search_word <- "m.d"

# ─────────────────────────────────────────

# Define exact essay columns to avoid picking up flag columns
essay_cols <- paste0("essay", 0:9)

df %>%
  mutate(all_essays = apply(dplyr::select(., all_of(essay_cols)),
                            1, paste, collapse = " ")) %>%
  filter(str_detect(all_essays,
                    regex(search_word, ignore_case = TRUE))) %>%
  slice_sample(n = 50) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = all_of(essay_cols),
               names_to = "essay_prompt",
               values_to = "response") %>%
  filter(str_detect(response,
                    regex(search_word, ignore_case = TRUE)),
         !is.na(response)) %>%
  mutate(
    essay_prompt = recode(essay_prompt,
      "essay0" = "Self Summary",
      "essay1" = "What I'm Doing With My Life",
      "essay2" = "I'm Really Good At",
      "essay3" = "First Thing People Notice",
      "essay4" = "Favorite Books/Movies/Music",
      "essay5" = "Six Things I Can't Live Without",
      "essay6" = "I Spend Thinking About",
      "essay7" = "Typical Friday Night",
      "essay8" = "Most Private Thing",
      "essay9" = "You Should Message Me If"
    ),
    response = str_remove_all(response, "<[^>]+>"),
    response = str_remove_all(response, "&[a-z]+;"),
    context  = str_extract(response,
                           paste0(".{0,80}",
                                  search_word,
                                  ".{0,80}"))
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(age, sex, ethnicity, education,
                essay_prompt, context) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Age", "Sex", "Ethnicity",
                  "Education", "Essay Prompt",
                  "Context (80 chars around match)"),
    caption = paste0("50 sample responses containing: '",
                     search_word, "'")
  )
```
### Geographic breakdown:  
```{r}
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Geographic Breakdown of OKCupid Dataset
# ─────────────────────────────────────────

# ── Step 1: Parse city and state ───────────

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    city  = str_trim(str_extract(location, "^[^,]+")),
    state = str_trim(str_extract(location, "(?<=,\\s).*$"))
  )

# ── Step 2: City breakdown ─────────────────

df %>%
  count(city, sort = TRUE) %>%
  filter(!is.na(city)) %>%
  mutate(pct = round(n / nrow(df) * 100, 2)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("City", "Count", "% of Profiles"),
    caption   = "Profile Count by City"
  )

# ── Step 3: State breakdown ─────────────────

df %>%
  count(state, sort = TRUE) %>%
  filter(!is.na(state)) %>%
  mutate(pct = round(n / nrow(df) * 100, 2)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("State", "Count", "% of Profiles"),
    caption   = "Profile Count by State"
  )
```
### Response Counts: 

```{r}
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Response count for each essay prompt
# ─────────────────────────────────────────

essay_cols <- paste0("essay", 0:9)

essay_labels <- c(
  "essay0" = "Self Summary",
  "essay1" = "What I'm Doing With My Life",
  "essay2" = "I'm Really Good At",
  "essay3" = "First Thing People Notice",
  "essay4" = "Favorite Books/Movies/Music",
  "essay5" = "Six Things I Can't Live Without",
  "essay6" = "I Spend Thinking About",
  "essay7" = "Typical Friday Night",
  "essay8" = "Most Private Thing",
  "essay9" = "You Should Message Me If"
)

map_dfr(essay_cols, function(e) {
  tibble(
    essay         = essay_labels[e],
    total         = nrow(df),
    responded     = sum(!is.na(df[[e]]) & 
                        str_trim(df[[e]]) != "", 
                        na.rm = TRUE),
    no_response   = total - responded,
    pct_responded = round(responded / total * 100, 1)
  )
}) %>%
  arrange(desc(responded)) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Essay Prompt", "Total Profiles",
                  "Responded", "No Response", 
                  "% Responded"),
    caption = "Response Rate by Essay Prompt"
  )
```
# Variable Visualizations 
## Section A: Univariate Variable Visualizations 
### Age: 
```{r}
ggplot(df, aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, color = "black", fill = "skyblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Histogram of Age",
    subtitle = "Shape: slightly right-skewed | Center: late 20s | Spread: ~18–70 | Possible high-age outliers",
    x = "Age",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_age.png",
  bg = "white"
)
```
### Relationship Status: 
```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    status = fct_explicit_na(status, na_level = "Missing"),
    status = fct_lump_prop(status, prop = 0.01, other_level = "Other")
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_infreq(status))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Bar Chart of Relationship Status",
    subtitle = "Most users are single; rare categories grouped into 'Other'",
    x = "Status",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

  ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_status.png",
  bg = "white"
)
```
### Sex: 
```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    sex = fct_explicit_na(as.factor(sex), na_level = "Missing"),
    sex = fct_lump_prop(sex, prop = 0.01, other_level = "Other")
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_infreq(sex))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Bar Chart of Sex",
    subtitle = "Most users identify as male or female; very small categories grouped into 'Other'",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

  ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_sex.png",
  bg = "white"
)
```
### Orientation: 
```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    orientation = fct_explicit_na(as.factor(orientation), na_level = "Missing"),
    orientation = fct_lump_prop(orientation, prop = 0.01, other_level = "Other")
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_infreq(orientation))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Bar Chart of Orientation",
    subtitle = "Most users identify as straight; smaller groups include gay and bisexual, with rare categories grouped into 'Other'",
    x = "Orientation",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_orientation.png",
  bg = "white"
)
```
### Body Type: 
```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    body_type = fct_explicit_na(as.factor(body_type), na_level = "Missing"),
    body_type = fct_lump_prop(body_type, prop = 0.01, other_level = "Other")
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_infreq(body_type))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Bar Chart of Body Type",
    subtitle = "Many categories present; less common body types grouped into 'Other', with some missing values",
    x = "Body Type",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_bodytype.png",
  bg = "white" 
)
```
### Diet: 
```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    diet = fct_explicit_na(as.factor(diet), na_level = "Missing"),
    diet = fct_lump_prop(diet, prop = 0.01, other_level = "Other")
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_infreq(diet))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Bar Chart of Diet",
    subtitle = "High number of missing responses; rare diet categories grouped into 'Other'",
    x = "Diet",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_diet.png",
  bg = "white" 
)


```
### Drinks: 
```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    drinks = fct_explicit_na(as.factor(drinks), na_level = "Missing"),
    drinks = fct_lump_prop(drinks, prop = 0.01, other_level = "Other")
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_infreq(drinks))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Bar Chart of Drinks",
    subtitle = "Most users report drinking socially; a few rare categories grouped into 'Other'",
    x = "Drinks",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))


ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_drinks.png",
  bg = "white" 
)
```
### Drug Usage: 
```{r}

univariate_drugs <- df %>%
  mutate(
    drugs = fct_explicit_na(
      factor(drugs, levels = c("never", "sometimes", "often")),
      na_level = "Did not answer"
    )
  ) %>%
  count(drugs) %>%
  mutate(
    total = sum(n),
    pct = n / total,
    se = sqrt(pct * (1 - pct) / n),
    ci_low = pmax(0, pct - 1.96 * se),
    ci_high = pmin(1, pct + 1.96 * se),
    drugs_label = paste0(
      tools::toTitleCase(as.character(drugs)),
      "\n(n = ", comma(n), ")"
    )
  )

ggplot(univariate_drugs, aes(x = drugs_label, y = pct, fill = drugs)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +

  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    width = 0.15,
    size = 0.8, 
    linewidth = 0.5, 
    color = "darkgrey"
  ) +
  
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)),
    vjust = -0.6,
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 5,
    family = "serif"
  ) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, max(univariate_drugs$ci_high) + 0.05)
  ) +
  
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "never" = "#96CDCD",
      "sometimes" = "#1E90FF",
      "often" = "#00008B",
      "Did not answer" = "#4A708B"
    )
  ) +

  labs(
    title = "Drug Use Among Respondents",
    subtitle = "Distribution of self-reported drug use (95% CI)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "% of Respondents",
        caption = "Data source: OkCupid data from 2015"

  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, family = "serif"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "italic", color = "gray40", family = "serif"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(family = "serif"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(family = "serif"),
    axis.text.y = element_text(family = "serif"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(), 
    plot.caption = element_text(face = "italic", color = "black", family = "serif", size = 10)

  )


ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_drug_use.png",
  bg = "white"
)
```
### Education Level: 
```{r}
clean_educ <- df %>% 
  mutate(
    education = ifelse(is.na(education) | education == "", "Did not answer", education)
  ) %>% 
  count(education) %>% 
  mutate(pct = scales::percent(n / sum(n), accuracy = 0.1)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(n))

clean_educ

univariate_educ <- df %>%
  mutate(
    education = ifelse(is.na(education) | education == "", "Did not answer", education),
    educ_group = case_when(
      education == "Did not answer" ~ "Did not answer",
      
      education %in% c(
        "graduated from high school",
        "high school",
        "working on high school",
        "dropped out of high school"
      ) ~ "High school or less",
      
      education %in% c(
        "working on college/university",
        "dropped out of college/university",
        "college/university",
        "graduated from two-year college",
        "working on two-year college",
        "two-year college",
        "dropped out of two-year college"
      ) ~ "Some college / associate",
      
      education == "graduated from college/university" ~ "Bachelor's",
      
      education %in% c(
        "working on masters program",
        "graduated from masters program",
        "dropped out of masters program",
        "masters program",
        "working on ph.d program",
        "graduated from ph.d program",
        "dropped out of ph.d program",
        "ph.d program",
        "working on law school",
        "graduated from law school",
        "dropped out of law school",
        "law school",
        "working on med school",
        "graduated from med school",
        "dropped out of med school",
        "med school"
      ) ~ "Graduate / professional",
      
      education %in% c(
        "graduated from space camp",
        "dropped out of space camp",
        "working on space camp",
        "space camp"
      ) ~ "Other / space camp",
      
      TRUE ~ "Other / space camp"
    )
  ) %>%
  count(educ_group) %>%
  mutate(
    total = sum(n),
    pct = n / total,
    se = sqrt(pct * (1 - pct) / n),
    ci_low = pmax(0, pct - 1.96 * se),
    ci_high = pmin(1, pct + 1.96 * se),

    educ_label = paste0(
      educ_group,
      "\n(n = ", scales::comma(n), ")"
    ),

    # reorder BOTH labels and colors by pct
    educ_label = forcats::fct_reorder(educ_label, pct),
    educ_group = forcats::fct_reorder(educ_group, pct)
  )

univariate_educ

ggplot(univariate_educ, aes(x = educ_label, y = pct, fill = educ_group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.5,
    color = "gray50"
  ) +
  
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)),
    vjust = -0.45,
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 4,
    family = "serif"
  ) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, max(univariate_educ$ci_high) + 0.06),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
  ) +
  
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  
  labs(
    title = "Education Distribution of Respondents",
    subtitle = "Grouped education levels (95% CI)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "% of Respondents",
    caption = "Data source: OkCupid data from 2015"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, family = "serif", size = 24),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "italic", color = "gray40", family = "serif", size = 18),
    axis.title.y = element_text(family = "serif", size = 18),
    axis.text.x = element_text(
      family = "serif",
      size = 10,
      angle = 20,
      hjust = 1,
      lineheight = 0.9,
      margin = margin(t = 10)
    ),
    axis.text.y = element_text(family = "serif", size = 14),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.margin = margin(15, 20, 20, 20),
    plot.caption = element_text(
      face = "italic",
      color = "black",
      family = "serif",
      size = 10,
      hjust = 0
    )
  )

ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_education_grouped.png",
  bg = "white"
)
```
### Ethnicity: 
```{r}
univariate_ethnicity <- df %>%
  mutate(
    ethnicity = case_when(
      is.na(ethnicity) | ethnicity == "" ~ "Did not answer",
      str_detect(ethnicity, ",") ~ "multiple",
      TRUE ~ ethnicity
    ),
    ethnicity = as.factor(ethnicity)
  ) %>%
  count(ethnicity) %>%
  mutate(
    total = sum(n),
    pct = n / total,
    se = sqrt(pct * (1 - pct) / n),
    ci_low = pmax(0, pct - 1.96 * se),
    ci_high = pmin(1, pct + 1.96 * se),
    ethnicity_clean = case_when(
      ethnicity == "asian" ~ "Asian",
      ethnicity == "black" ~ "Black",
      ethnicity == "hispanic / latin" ~ "Hispanic / Latino",
      ethnicity == "indian" ~ "Indian",
      ethnicity == "middle eastern" ~ "Middle Eastern",
      ethnicity == "native american" ~ "Native American",
      ethnicity == "other" ~ "Other",
      ethnicity == "pacific islander" ~ "Pacific Islander",
      ethnicity == "multiple" ~ "Multiple",
      ethnicity == "white" ~ "White",
      ethnicity == "Did not answer" ~ "Did not answer",
      TRUE ~ tools::toTitleCase(as.character(ethnicity))
    ),
    n_label = paste0("n = ", comma(n), "")
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    ethnicity_clean = factor(
      ethnicity_clean,
      levels = ethnicity_clean[order(pct)]
    )
  )

ggplot(univariate_ethnicity, aes(x = ethnicity_clean, y = pct, fill = ethnicity_clean)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.5,
    color = "gray50"
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)),
    vjust = -0.45,
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 4,
    family = "serif"
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(y = -0.018, label = n_label),
    size = 3,
    family = "serif",
    color = "black"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(-0.035, max(univariate_ethnicity$ci_high) + 0.06),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  labs(
    title = "Ethnicity Distribution of Respondents",
    subtitle = "Distribution of self-reported ethnicity (95% CI)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "% of Respondents", 
    caption = "Data source: OkCupid data from 2015"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, family = "serif", size = 24),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "italic", color = "gray40", family = "serif", size = 18),
    axis.title.y = element_text(family = "serif", size = 18),
    axis.text.x = element_text(
      family = "serif",
      size = 10,
      angle = 20,
      hjust = 1,
      lineheight = 0.9,
      margin = margin(t = 10)
    ),
    axis.text.y = element_text(family = "serif", size = 14),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.margin = margin(15, 20, 40, 20),
    plot.caption = element_text(
      face = "italic",
      color = "black",
      family = "serif",
      size = 10,
      hjust = 0
    )
  )

ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_ethnicity.png",
  bg = "white"
)
```
### Height: 
```{r}
clean_height <- df %>% 
  mutate(
    height = ifelse(is.na(height) | height == "", "Did not \nanswer", as.character(height))
  ) %>% 
  count(height) %>% 
  mutate(pct = scales::percent(n / sum(n), accuracy = 0.1)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(n))

clean_height

univariate_height <- df %>%
  mutate(
    height = ifelse(is.na(height) | height == "", "Did not \nanswer", as.character(height)),
    height_num = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(height)),
    
    height_group = case_when(
      height == "Did not \nanswer" ~ "Did not \nanswer",
      is.na(height_num) ~ "Did not \nanswer",
      height_num < 48 | height_num > 84 ~ "Outlier",
      height_num < 60 ~ "Under 5'0\"",
      height_num >= 60 & height_num <= 63 ~ "5'0\"–5'3\"\n(60–63 in)",
      height_num >= 64 & height_num <= 67 ~ "5'4\"–5'7\"\n(64–67 in)",
      height_num >= 68 & height_num <= 71 ~ "5'8\"–5'11\"\n(68–71 in)",
      height_num >= 72 & height_num <= 75 ~ "6'0\"–6'3\"\n(72–75 in)",
      height_num >= 76 ~ "6'4\"+"
    )
  ) %>%
  count(height_group) %>%
  mutate(
    total = sum(n),
    pct = n / total,
    se = sqrt(pct * (1 - pct) / total),
    ci_low = pmax(0, pct - 1.96 * se),
    ci_high = pmin(1, pct + 1.96 * se),
    
    height_group = factor(
      height_group,
      levels = c(
        "Under 5'0\"",
        "5'0\"–5'3\"\n(60–63 in)",
        "5'4\"–5'7\"\n(64–67 in)",
        "5'8\"–5'11\"\n(68–71 in)",
        "6'0\"–6'3\"\n(72–75 in)",
        "6'4\"+",
        "Outlier",
        "Did not \nanswer"
      )
    )
  ) %>%
  arrange(height_group) %>%
  mutate(
    height_label = paste0(as.character(height_group), "\n n = ", scales::comma(n))
  )

ggplot(univariate_height, aes(x = height_group, y = pct, fill = height_group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.5,
    color = "gray50"
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)),
    vjust = -0.45,
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 4,
    family = "serif"
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = univariate_height$height_label) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(0, max(univariate_height$ci_high) + 0.06),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  labs(
    title = "Height Distribution of Respondents",
    subtitle = "Grouped self-reported height (95% CI)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "% of Respondents",
    caption = "Data source: OkCupid data from 2015"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, family = "serif", size = 24),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "italic", color = "gray40", family = "serif", size = 18),
    axis.title.y = element_text(family = "serif", size = 18),
    axis.text.x = element_text(
      family = "serif",
      size = 10,
      lineheight = 0.9,
      margin = margin(t = 10)
    ),
    axis.text.y = element_text(family = "serif", size = 14),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.margin = margin(15, 20, 20, 20),
    plot.caption = element_text(
      face = "italic",
      color = "black",
      family = "serif",
      size = 10,
      hjust = 0
    )
  )

ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_height_grouped.png",
  bg = "white"
)
```
### Income: 
```{r}
clean_income <- df %>% 
  mutate(
    income = ifelse(is.na(income) | income == "", "Did not \nanswer", as.character(income))
  ) %>% 
  count(income) %>% 
  mutate(pct = scales::percent(n / sum(n), accuracy = 0.1)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(n))

clean_income
```
### Job Category: 
```{r}
clean_job <- df %>% 
  mutate(
    job = ifelse(is.na(job) | job == "", "Did not \nanswer", as.character(job))
  ) %>% 
  count(job) %>% 
  mutate(pct = scales::percent(n / sum(n), accuracy = 0.1)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(n))

clean_job

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------#

univariate_job <- df %>%
  mutate(
    job_clean = str_to_lower(job),
    
    job_group = case_when(
      job_clean == "student" ~ "Student",
      
      job_clean %in% c(
        "science / tech / engineering",
        "computer / hardware / software"
      ) ~ "Computers / Tech / Engineering",
      
      job_clean %in% c(
        "sales / marketing / biz dev",
        "banking / financial / real estate"
      ) ~ "Business / Finance / Sales",
      
      job_clean %in% c(
        "artistic / musical / writer",
        "entertainment / media"
      ) ~ "Entertainment / Art / Media",
      
      job_clean == "medicine / health" ~ "Health / Medicine",
      job_clean == "education / academia" ~ "Education / Research",
      
      job_clean %in% c(
        "political / government",
        "military"
      ) ~ "Government / Military",
      
      job_clean == "law / legal services" ~ "Law / Legal",
      
      job_clean %in% c(
        "construction / craftsmanship",
        "transportation"
      ) ~ "Construction / Transportation",
      
      job_clean %in% c(
        "clerical / administrative",
        "hospitality / travel"
      ) ~ "Admin / Service",
      
      job_clean %in% c("unemployed", "retired") ~ "Not in Labor Force",
      
      job_clean %in% c("other", "rather not say", "did not answer") ~ "Other / Missing",
      is.na(job_clean) ~ "Other / Missing",
      TRUE ~ "Other / Missing"
    )
  ) %>%
  count(job_group) %>%
  mutate(
    total = sum(n),
    pct = n / total,
    se = sqrt(pct * (1 - pct) / total),
    ci_low = pmax(0, pct - 1.96 * se),
    ci_high = pmin(1, pct + 1.96 * se)
  ) %>%
  arrange(n) %>%
  mutate(
    job_group = factor(job_group, levels = job_group),
    job_title = case_when(
  job_group == "Computers / Tech / Engineering" ~ "Computers / Tech / Engineering",
  job_group == "Business / Finance / Sales" ~ "Business / Finance / Sales",
  job_group == "Entertainment / Art / Media" ~ "Entertainment / Art / Media",
  job_group == "Government / Military" ~ "Government / Military",
  job_group == "Construction / Transportation" ~ "Construction / Transportation",
  job_group == "Admin / Service" ~ "Admin / Service",
  job_group == "Not in Labor Force" ~ "Not in Labor Force",
  job_group == "Other / Missing" ~ "Other / Missing",
  TRUE ~ job_group
),
    n_label = paste0("n = ", comma(n))
  )

#---------------------------

ggplot(univariate_job, aes(x = job_group, y = pct, fill = job_group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_low, ymax = ci_high),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.5,
    color = "gray50"
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)),
    vjust = -0.45,
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 4,
    family = "serif"
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(y = -0.012, label = n_label),
    angle = 0,
    size = 3,
    family = "serif",
    vjust = 1
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = univariate_job$job_title) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(-0.04, max(univariate_job$ci_high) + 0.06),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.02))
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  labs(
    title = "Job Titles of Respondents",
    subtitle = "Grouped self-reported job title (95% CI)",
    x = NULL,
    y = "% of Respondents",
    caption = "Data source: OkCupid data from 2015"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, family = "serif", size = 24),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "italic", color = "gray40", family = "serif", size = 18),
    axis.title.y = element_text(family = "serif", size = 18),
    axis.text.x = element_text(
      family = "serif",
      size = 10,
      angle = 20,
      hjust = 1,
      vjust = 1,
      lineheight = 0.9,
      margin = margin(t = 28)
    ),
    axis.text.y = element_text(family = "serif", size = 14),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.margin = margin(15, 20, 45, 20),
    plot.caption = element_text(
      face = "italic",
      color = "black",
      family = "serif",
      size = 10,
      hjust = 0
    )
  )

ggsave(
  filename = "Univariate_jobtitles_grouped.png",
  bg = "white"
)
```
### Last Online: 
Note: We decided to not use this variable since we can not analyze it in a significant and meaningful manner. We do not have the exact information of when the data was pulled to create the dataset, so we are unable to contextualize the timestamps of when each person was last online. 
### Location: 
```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    state = str_extract(location, "[^,]+$") %>% str_trim() %>% str_to_lower(),
    city  = str_extract(location, "^[^,]+") %>% str_trim() %>% str_to_title()
  ) %>%
  add_count(city, name = "n_city") %>%
  mutate(
    rank  = dense_rank(desc(n_city)),
    label = case_when(
      rank <= 15            ~ city,
      state == "california" ~ "Other California",
      TRUE                  ~ "Other"
    )
  ) %>%
  count(label, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    label = fct_reorder(label, n),
    label = fct_relevel(label, "Other California", "Other", after = 0)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = label, y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#4B8BBE") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title   = "OKCupid Users by Location",
    x       = "Location",
    y       = "Count",
    caption = "Figure: Top 15 cities by user count. Remaining California cities grouped as 'Other California'; all non-California users grouped as 'Other'. Data: OKCupid profiles dataset."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Times New Roman") +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text    = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title   = element_text(size = 11),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, face = "italic", family = "Times New Roman")
  )

ggsave(
  filename = "Figure6_LocationDistribution.png",
  plot     = last_plot(),
  bg       = "white"
)
```
### Offspring: 

```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    offspring_group = case_when(
      str_detect(offspring, "^has a kid|^has kids")                          ~ "Has Kids",
      str_detect(offspring, "wants them|wants kids|might want kids|might want them") ~ "Wants Kids",
      str_detect(offspring, "doesn't want kids|doesn't want any|doesn't want more") ~ "Doesn't Want Kids",
      str_detect(offspring, "^doesn't have kids")                            ~ "No Kids (Undecided)",
      TRUE                                                                   ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(offspring_group)) %>%
  count(offspring_group, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(offspring_group = fct_reorder(offspring_group, n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = offspring_group, y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#4B8BBE") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title   = "OKCupid Users by Offspring Preference",
    x       = "Group",
    y       = "Count",
    caption = "Figure: Offspring responses collapsed into four groups. Blank responses excluded. The distribution is right-skewed, with the largest share of users either wanting kids or having none yet with no stated preference, while those who already have kids represent a smaller but notable segment. Data: OKCupid profiles dataset."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Times New Roman") +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text    = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title   = element_text(size = 11),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, face = "italic", family = "Times New Roman")
  )

ggsave(
  filename = "Figure7_OffspringPreference.png",
  plot     = last_plot(),
  bg       = "white"
)
```
### Pets: 
```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    pets_group = case_when(
      str_detect(pets, "dislikes dogs") & str_detect(pets, "dislikes cats")                    ~ "Dislikes Both",
      str_detect(pets, "dislikes dogs") | str_detect(pets, "dislikes cats")                    ~ "Dislikes Pets",
      str_detect(pets, "likes dogs") & str_detect(pets, "likes cats")                          ~ "Likes Both",
      str_detect(pets, "likes dogs") & !str_detect(pets, "likes cats|has cats")                ~ "Likes Dogs Only",
      str_detect(pets, "likes cats") & !str_detect(pets, "likes dogs|has dogs")                ~ "Likes Cats Only",
      str_detect(pets, "has dogs|has cats")                                                    ~ "Has Pets",
      TRUE                                                                                     ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(pets_group)) %>%
  count(pets_group, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(pets_group = fct_reorder(pets_group, n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = pets_group, y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#4B8BBE") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title   = "OKCupid Users by Pet Preference",
    x       = "Group",
    y       = "Count",
    caption = "Figure: Pet responses collapsed into six groups. Blank responses excluded. The distribution is right-skewed, with 'Likes Both' dominating; users who actively dislike pets are the smallest group, suggesting strong positive pet sentiment across this sample. Data: OKCupid profiles dataset."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Times New Roman") +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text    = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title   = element_text(size = 11),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, face = "italic", family = "Times New Roman")
  )

ggsave(
  filename = here("Figure8_PetPreference.png"),
  plot     = last_plot(),
  bg       = "white"
)
```
### Religion: 
```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    religion_group = case_when(
      str_detect(religion, "^agnosticism") ~ "Agnostic",
      str_detect(religion, "^atheism")     ~ "Atheist",
      str_detect(religion, "^buddhism")    ~ "Buddhist",
      str_detect(religion, "^catholicism") ~ "Catholic",
      str_detect(religion, "^christianity")~ "Christian",
      str_detect(religion, "^hinduism")    ~ "Hindu",
      str_detect(religion, "^islam")       ~ "Muslim",
      str_detect(religion, "^judaism")     ~ "Jewish",
      str_detect(religion, "^other")       ~ "Other",
      TRUE                                 ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(religion_group)) %>%
  count(religion_group, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(religion_group = fct_reorder(religion_group, n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = religion_group, y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#4B8BBE") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title   = "OKCupid Users by Religion",
    x       = "Religion",
    y       = "Count",
    caption = "Figure: Religion responses collapsed by faith tradition; seriousness qualifiers excluded. Blank responses excluded. Data: OKCupid profiles dataset."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Times New Roman") +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text    = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title   = element_text(size = 11),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, face = "italic", family = "Times New Roman")
  )

ggsave(
  filename = here("Figure9_Religion.png"),
  plot     = last_plot(),
  bg       = "white"
)
```
### Astrological Sign: 
```{r}
df %>%
  mutate(
    sign = case_when(
      str_detect(sign, "^aquarius")   ~ "Aquarius",
      str_detect(sign, "^aries")      ~ "Aries",
      str_detect(sign, "^cancer")     ~ "Cancer",
      str_detect(sign, "^capricorn")  ~ "Capricorn",
      str_detect(sign, "^gemini")     ~ "Gemini",
      str_detect(sign, "^leo")        ~ "Leo",
      str_detect(sign, "^libra")      ~ "Libra",
      str_detect(sign, "^pisces")     ~ "Pisces",
      str_detect(sign, "^sagittarius")~ "Sagittarius",
      str_detect(sign, "^scorpio")    ~ "Scorpio",
      str_detect(sign, "^taurus")     ~ "Taurus",
      str_detect(sign, "^virgo")      ~ "Virgo",
      TRUE                            ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(sign)) %>%
  count(sign, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(sign = fct_reorder(sign, n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = sign, y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#4B8BBE") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title   = "OKCupid Users by Astrological Sign",
    x       = "Sign",
    y       = "Count",
    caption = "Figure: Astrological sign responses collapsed by sign; attitude qualifiers excluded. Blank responses excluded. The distribution is remarkably uniform across all 12 signs, consistent with roughly equal birth rate distribution across months, with no sign standing out as a clear outlier. Data: OKCupid profiles dataset."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Times New Roman") +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text    = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title   = element_text(size = 11),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, face = "italic", family = "Times New Roman")
  )

ggsave(
  filename = here("Figure10_AstrologicalSign.png"),
  plot     = last_plot(),
  bg       = "white"
)
```
### Smokes: 
```{r}
df %>%
  filter(smokes != "" & !is.na(smokes)) %>%
  mutate(smokes = str_to_title(smokes)) %>%
  count(smokes, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(smokes = fct_reorder(smokes, n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = smokes, y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#4B8BBE") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title   = "OKCupid Users by Smoking Status",
    x       = "Smoking Status",
    y       = "Count",
    caption = "Figure: Smoking status responses shown as-is; blank responses excluded. Data: OKCupid profiles dataset."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Times New Roman") +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text    = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title   = element_text(size = 11),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, face = "italic", family = "Times New Roman")
  )

ggsave(
  filename = here("Figure11_SmokingStatus.png"),
  plot     = last_plot(),
  width    = 10,
  height   = 7,
  dpi      = 300,
  bg       = "white"
)
```
### Languages Spoken: 
```{r}
languages <- c("spanish", "french", "mandarin", "chinese", "japanese", "german",
               "italian", "portuguese", "korean", "arabic", "russian", "tagalog",
               "hindi", "hebrew", "sign language")

df %>%
  mutate(speaks_clean = str_to_lower(speaks)) %>%
  mutate(
    # check for each major language at each fluency level
    lang_group = case_when(
      # fluent in any major language (highest priority)
      str_detect(speaks_clean, paste(paste0(languages, " \\(fluently\\)"), collapse = "|")) ~
        str_extract(speaks_clean, paste(paste0(languages, " \\(fluently\\)"), collapse = "|")) %>%
        str_remove(" \\(fluently\\)") %>% str_to_title(),
      # okay in any major language
      str_detect(speaks_clean, paste(paste0(languages, " \\(okay\\)"), collapse = "|")) ~
        str_extract(speaks_clean, paste(paste0(languages, " \\(okay\\)"), collapse = "|")) %>%
        str_remove(" \\(okay\\)") %>% str_to_title(),
      # poorly in any major language
      str_detect(speaks_clean, paste(paste0(languages, " \\(poorly\\)"), collapse = "|")) ~
        str_extract(speaks_clean, paste(paste0(languages, " \\(poorly\\)"), collapse = "|")) %>%
        str_remove(" \\(poorly\\)") %>% str_to_title(),
      # english only
      str_detect(speaks_clean, "english") ~ "English Only",
      speaks_clean == "" | is.na(speaks_clean) ~ NA_character_,
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(lang_group)) %>%
  count(lang_group, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    lang_group = case_when(
      n < 300 ~ "Other",
      TRUE    ~ lang_group
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(lang_group) %>%
  summarise(n = sum(n)) %>%
  mutate(
    lang_group = fct_reorder(lang_group, n),
    lang_group = fct_relevel(lang_group, "Other", after = 0)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = lang_group, y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#4B8BBE") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title   = "OKCupid Users by Language (Best Non-English Language)",
    x       = "Language",
    y       = "Count",
    caption = "Figure: Users classified by their highest-proficiency non-English language (fluent > okay > poorly). English-only users grouped separately. Languages spoken by fewer than 300 users collapsed into 'Other'. Blank responses excluded. Data: OKCupid profiles dataset."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Times New Roman") +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.text    = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title   = element_text(size = 11),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, face = "italic", family = "Times New Roman")
  )

ggsave(
  filename = here("Figure12_Language.png"),
  plot     = last_plot(),
  width    = 10,
  height   = 7,
  dpi      = 300,
  bg       = "white"
)
```
## Section B: Bivariate Visualizations 

### Higher Education & Preference for Educated Partner: 
```{r}
library(extrafont)
loadfonts(quiet = TRUE)

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    # Clean essay9 first
    essay9_clean = str_remove_all(essay9, "<[^>]+>"),
    essay9_clean = str_remove_all(essay9_clean, "&[a-z]+;"),

    # Educated partner signal
    signals_educated_partner = str_detect(
      essay9_clean,
      regex("intelligent|educated|smart|ambitious|intellectual|curious|well-read|driven|thoughtful|articulate",
            ignore_case = TRUE)
    ),

    # Education tier
    edu_tier = case_when(
      str_detect(education, regex("ph\\.?d|master|law school|med school|doctoral", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "High",
      str_detect(education, regex("college|university|bachelor", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Medium",
      str_detect(education, regex("high school|two-year|dropped out", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "Low",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    edu_tier = factor(edu_tier, levels = c("Low", "Medium", "High"))
  )

# ── Prep data with sample sizes and CIs ────

```









This figure shows the percentage of OKCupid respondents whose partner preference essay contained at least one education-signaling keyword, broken down by respondent education level and gender. Higher education is associated with stronger preference signaling for an educated partner across both sexes, with highly educated female respondents signaling at the highest rate (15.4\%) and the gradient being steeper among women than men. This raises the question of whether the gender asymmetry reflects differential returns to education in the marriage market, or whether highly educated women face a smaller pool of equally educated partners and thus signal more explicitly to filter candidates.

### Religious Identity & Preference for Partner's Faith: 
```{r}
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Religion signaling keywords in Essay 9
# by self-reported religion
# ─────────────────────────────────────────

# ── Step 1: Religion signal keywords ───────

religion_signal_keywords <- c(
  # Explicit preference language
  "religious", "faith", "faithful", "devout", "practicing",
  "spiritual", "god-fearing", "god fearing", "church",
  "mosque", "synagogue", "temple", "worship",

  # Partner faith preference
  "shares my faith", "share my faith", "same religion",
  "same faith", "similar beliefs", "similar values",
  "believes in god", "belief in god", "christian",
  "muslim", "jewish", "catholic", "buddhist", "hindu",
  "agnostic", "atheist",

  # Practice signals
  "pray", "prayer", "bible", "quran", "torah",
  "sabbath", "mass", "halal", "kosher", "meditate",
  "meditation", "spirituality"
)

religion_signal_pattern <- paste(religion_signal_keywords,
                                  collapse = "|")

# ── Step 2: Clean religion field and
#    add signal flag ──────────────────────

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    essay9_clean = str_remove_all(essay9, "<[^>]+>"),
    essay9_clean = str_remove_all(essay9_clean, "&[a-z]+;"),
    religion_simple = str_extract(
      religion,
      regex("agnosticism|atheism|buddhism|catholicism|
             christianity|hinduism|islam|judaism|other",
            ignore_case = TRUE)
    ),
    religion_simple = str_to_title(religion_simple),
    signals_religion_partner = str_detect(
      essay9_clean,
      regex(religion_signal_pattern, ignore_case = TRUE)
    )
  )

# ── Step 3: Prep plot data ──────────────────

plot_data_rel <- df %>%
  filter(!is.na(religion_simple),
         !is.na(signals_religion_partner)) %>%
  group_by(religion_simple) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mentions = sum(signals_religion_partner, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct      = mentions / n * 100,
    .groups  = "drop"
  )

# ── Step 4: Plot ────────────────────────────

plot_data_rel %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(religion_simple, pct),
             y = pct,
             fill = pct)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%  (n = ",
                               scales::comma(n), ")")),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5,
            family = "Times New Roman",
            fontface = "bold") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    colors = c("#96CDCD", "#1E90FF", "#00008B")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, max(plot_data_rel$pct) * 1.4),
    breaks = seq(0,
                 ceiling(max(plot_data_rel$pct) * 1.4 / 5) * 5,
                 by = 5),
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Religious Identity Predicts Partner Faith Preference Signaling",
    subtitle = "Actively religious groups signal faith-based partner preferences at far higher rates",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "% of Profiles Signaling Religion-Based Partner Preference",
    caption  = paste0(
      "Figure 2. Percentage of OKCupid respondents by self-reported religion whose partner\n",
      "preference essay (Essay 9) contained at least one religion-signaling keyword.\n",
      "Keywords include: religious, faithful, devout, spiritual, church, mosque, synagogue,\n",
      "shares my faith, same religion, pray, bible, quran, torah, and related terms.\n",
      "Sample sizes shown in parentheses beside each percentage. ",
      "Data source: OKCupid public use dataset, San Francisco Bay Area, 2012."
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Times New Roman") +
  theme(
    plot.title            = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12,
                                         hjust = 0.5,
                                         margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle         = element_text(size = 9.5, hjust = 0.5,
                                         color = "grey30",
                                         lineheight = 1.4,
                                         face = "italic",
                                         margin = margin(b = 12)),
    axis.title.x          = element_text(size = 10,
                                         margin = margin(t = 10)),
    axis.title.y          = element_text(size = 10,
                                         margin = margin(r = 10)),
    axis.text             = element_text(size = 10, color = "black"),
    panel.grid.major.y    = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor      = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x    = element_line(color = "grey90",
                                         linewidth = 0.4),
    plot.caption          = element_text(size = 8, hjust = 0,
                                         color = "grey20",
                                         lineheight = 1.6,
                                         face = "plain",
                                         margin = margin(t = 16, l = 10)),
    plot.caption.position = "plot",
    plot.margin           = margin(t = 16, r = 60, b = 16, l = 16),
    legend.position       = "none"
  )

ggsave(
  filename = "ReligionSignaling.png",
  plot     = last_plot(),
  width    = 10,
  height   = 6,
  dpi      = 300,
  bg       = "white"
)
```
This figure displays the rate at which respondents from each religious group explicitly signal a preference for a faith-matched partner in their Essay 9. Buddhist and Jewish respondents signal at the highest rates (both 3.2\%), while agnostic and Catholic respondents signal at the lowest, suggesting that minority or more culturally cohesive religious identities predict stronger in-group partner preference. This leads to the question of whether high-signaling groups are driven by genuine theological requirements for endogamy or by social and community pressures that make out-group partnership more costly.

### Age & Preference for a Mature Partner: 
```{r}
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Older partner preference signaling
# by age group
# ─────────────────────────────────────────

# ── Step 1: Older partner signal keywords ──

older_partner_keywords <- c(
  # Direct age preference
  "older", "mature", "older man", "older woman",
  "older guy", "older girl", "age gap", "age difference",
  "younger than me", "older than me",

  # Maturity signals
  "mature man", "mature woman", "mature person",
  "established", "settled", "knows what he wants",
  "knows what she wants", "knows what they want",
  "has his life together", "has her life together",
  "financially stable", "financially secure",
  "stable", "grounded", "seasoned",

  # Experience signals
  "experienced", "worldly", "been around",
  "life experience", "wisdom", "wise",

  # Explicit age mentions
  "40s", "50s", "60s", "40's", "50's", "60's",
  "over 40", "over 50", "over 35", "older partner",
  "silver fox", "cougar", "cub"
)

older_partner_pattern <- paste(older_partner_keywords,
                                collapse = "|")

# ── Step 2: Add age group and signal flag ──

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    essay9_clean = str_remove_all(essay9, "<[^>]+>"),
    essay9_clean = str_remove_all(essay9_clean, "&[a-z]+;"),
    age_group = case_when(
      age >= 18 & age <= 24 ~ "18-24",
      age >= 25 & age <= 29 ~ "25-29",
      age >= 30 & age <= 34 ~ "30-34",
      age >= 35 & age <= 39 ~ "35-39",
      age >= 40 & age <= 49 ~ "40-49",
      age >= 50             ~ "50+",
      TRUE                  ~ NA_character_
    ),
    age_group = factor(age_group, levels = c(
      "18-24", "25-29", "30-34",
      "35-39", "40-49", "50+"
    )),
    signals_older_partner = str_detect(
      essay9_clean,
      regex(older_partner_pattern, ignore_case = TRUE)
    )
  )

# ── Prep plot data with CIs ─────────────────

plot_data_age <- df %>%
  filter(!is.na(age_group),
         !is.na(signals_older_partner)) %>%
  group_by(age_group) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mentions = sum(signals_older_partner, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct      = mentions / n * 100,
    se       = sqrt((pct/100 * (1 - pct/100)) / n) * 100,
    ci_lower = pct - 1.96 * se,
    ci_upper = pct + 1.96 * se,
    .groups  = "drop"
  )

# ── Build x-axis labels with n= underneath ─

age_labels <- plot_data_age %>%
  mutate(label = paste0(age_group, "\n(n = ",
                        scales::comma(n), ")")) %>%
  dplyr::select(age_group, label) %>%
  deframe()

# ── Plot ────────────────────────────────────

plot_data_age %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = age_group, y = pct, fill = age_group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper),
                width = 0.08, color = "grey40",
                linewidth = 0.6) +
geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%"),
              y = ci_upper),
          vjust = -0.6, size = 3.8,
          family = "Times New Roman",
          fontface = "bold") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = age_labels) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "18-24" = "#BBDDFF",
    "25-29" = "#96CDCD",
    "30-34" = "#1E90FF",
    "35-39" = "#4A708B",
    "40-49" = "#00008B",
    "50+"   = "#7D7D7D"
  )) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, max(plot_data_age$ci_upper) * 1.35),
    breaks = seq(0,
                 ceiling(max(plot_data_age$ci_upper) * 1.35 / 2) * 2,
                 by = 2),
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.08))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Older Adults Are More Likely to Signal Preference for a Mature Partner",
    subtitle = "Rates of older partner preference signaling rise steadily with respondent age",
    x        = "Respondent Age Group",
    y        = "% of Profiles Signaling Older Partner Preference",
    caption  = paste0(
      "Figure 3. Percentage of OKCupid respondents by age group whose partner\n",
      "preference essay (Essay 9) contained at least one older-partner-signaling keyword.\n",
      "Keywords include: older, mature, age gap, established, stable, experienced,\n",
      "financially secure, wisdom, silver fox, and related terms.\n",
      "Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals. ",
      "Sample sizes shown in parentheses below each age group. ",
      "Data source: OKCupid public use dataset, San Francisco Bay Area, 2012."
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Times New Roman") +
  theme(
    plot.title            = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12,
                                         hjust = 0.5,
                                         margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle         = element_text(size = 9.5, hjust = 0.5,
                                         color = "grey30",
                                         lineheight = 1.4,
                                         face = "italic",
                                         margin = margin(b = 12)),
    axis.title.x          = element_text(size = 10,
                                         margin = margin(t = 10)),
    axis.title.y          = element_text(size = 10,
                                         margin = margin(r = 10)),
    axis.text.x           = element_text(size = 9, color = "black",
                                         lineheight = 1.4),
    axis.text.y           = element_text(size = 9, color = "black"),
    panel.grid.major.x    = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor      = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y    = element_line(color = "grey90",
                                         linewidth = 0.4),
    plot.caption          = element_text(size = 8, hjust = 0,
                                         color = "grey20",
                                         lineheight = 1.6,
                                         face = "plain",
                                         margin = margin(t = 16, l = 10)),
    plot.caption.position = "plot",
    plot.margin           = margin(t = 16, r = 16, b = 16, l = 16),
    legend.position       = "none"
  )

ggsave(
  filename = "OlderPartnerSignaling.png",
  plot     = last_plot(),
  width    = 10,
  height   = 6,
  dpi      = 300,
  bg       = "white"
)
```
This figure plots the share of respondents in each age group who signal a preference for a mature or older partner in their essay, revealing a near-monotonic increase from 1.6\% among 18-24 year olds to 4.3\% among 40-49 year olds. The pattern is consistent with life-stage explanations: older users may increasingly value markers of stability, financial security, and emotional maturity over youth-associated traits. This invites a follow-up question about whether the slight dip at 50+ reflects a ceiling effect, a shift toward same-age rather than older preferences, or simply smaller sample noise.

### Body Type & Preference for Partner's Appearance 
```{r}

# ── Step 0: Create partner_appearance_signal from Essay 9 ──

appearance_keywords <- c(
  "attractive", "good-looking", "good looking", "handsome",
  "beautiful", "fit body", "in shape", "toned", "physically fit",
  "fitness", "sexy", "appearance"
)

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    essay9_clean = tolower(essay9),
    partner_appearance_signal = str_detect(
      essay9_clean,
      paste(appearance_keywords, collapse = "|")
    )
  )
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Body type vs partner appearance signaling
# in Essay 9
# ─────────────────────────────────────────

# ── Step 1: Simplify body type groups ──────

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    body_type_simple = case_when(
      str_detect(body_type, "athletic|fit|jacked") ~ "Athletic/Fit/Jacked",
      str_detect(body_type, "thin|slim")           ~ "Thin/Slim",
      str_detect(body_type, "average")             ~ "Average",
      str_detect(body_type, "curvy")               ~ "Curvy",
      str_detect(body_type, "full|a little extra|overweight") ~ "Full/Overweight",
      str_detect(body_type, "used up")             ~ NA_character_,
      TRUE                                         ~ NA_character_
    ),
    body_type_simple = factor(body_type_simple, levels = c(
      "Athletic/Fit/Jacked", "Thin/Slim", "Average",
      "Curvy", "Full/Overweight"
    ))
  )

# ── Step 2: Prep plot data ──────────────────

plot_data_body <- df %>%
  filter(!is.na(body_type_simple),
         !is.na(partner_appearance_signal)) %>%
  group_by(body_type_simple) %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mentions = sum(partner_appearance_signal, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct      = mentions / n * 100,
    se       = sqrt((pct/100 * (1 - pct/100)) / n) * 100,
    ci_lower = pct - 1.96 * se,
    ci_upper = pct + 1.96 * se,
    .groups  = "drop"
  )

# ── Step 3: Build x-axis labels ────────────

body_labels <- plot_data_body %>%
  mutate(label = paste0(body_type_simple, "\n(n = ",
                        scales::comma(n), ")")) %>%
  dplyr::select(body_type_simple, label) %>%
  deframe()

# ── Step 4: Compute y-axis limit dynamically ──

y_max <- ceiling(max(plot_data_body$ci_upper) * 1.15 / 5) * 5

# ── Step 5: Plot ────────────────────────────

plot_data_body %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = body_type_simple, y = pct,
             fill = body_type_simple)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper),
                width = 0.08, color = "grey40",
                linewidth = 0.6) +
geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%"),
              y = ci_upper),
          vjust = -0.6, size = 3.8,
          family = "Times New Roman",
          fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Athletic/Fit/Jacked" = "#00008B",
    "Thin/Slim"           = "#1E90FF",
    "Average"             = "#96CDCD",
    "Curvy"               = "#BBDDFF",
    "Full/Overweight"     = "#7D7D7D"
  )) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = body_labels) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, y_max),
    breaks = seq(0, y_max, by = 5),
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Self-Reported Body Type Predicts Partner Appearance Preference Signaling",
    subtitle = "Athletic and fit users are more likely to explicitly seek physical fitness in a partner",
    x        = "Self-Reported Body Type",
    y        = "% Signaling Partner Appearance Preference",
    caption  = paste0(
      "Figure 5. Percentage of OKCupid respondents by self-reported body type whose\n",
      "partner preference essay (Essay 9) contained at least one physical appearance keyword.\n",
      "Athletic/Fit/Jacked combines jacked, athletic, and fit categories.\n",
      "Full/Overweight combines full-figured, a little extra, and overweight categories.\n",
      "The 'used up' body type category (n = ",
      scales::comma(sum(df$body_type == "used up", na.rm = TRUE)),
      ") was excluded from analysis.\n",
      "Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals. ",
      "Sample sizes shown in parentheses below each body type.\n",
      "Data source: OKCupid public use dataset, San Francisco Bay Area, 2012."
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_family = "Times New Roman") +
  theme(
    plot.title            = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12,
                                         hjust = 0.5,
                                         margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle         = element_text(size = 9.5, hjust = 0.5,
                                         color = "grey30",
                                         lineheight = 1.4,
                                         face = "italic",
                                         margin = margin(b = 12)),
    axis.title.x          = element_text(size = 10,
                                         margin = margin(t = 10)),
    axis.title.y          = element_text(size = 10,
                                         margin = margin(r = 10)),
    axis.text.x           = element_text(size = 9, color = "black",
                                         lineheight = 1.4),
    axis.text.y           = element_text(size = 9, color = "black"),
    panel.grid.major.x    = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor      = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y    = element_line(color = "grey90",
                                         linewidth = 0.4),
    plot.caption          = element_text(size = 8, hjust = 0,
                                         color = "grey20",
                                         lineheight = 1.8,
                                         face = "plain",
                                         margin = margin(t = 16, l = 10)),
    plot.caption.position = "plot",
    plot.margin           = margin(t = 16, r = 16, b = 16, l = 16),
    legend.position       = "none"
  )

ggsave(
  filename = "BodyTypeAppearanceSignaling.png",
  plot     = last_plot(),
  width    = 10,
  height   = 7,
  dpi      = 300,
  bg       = "white"
)
```
This figure shows that self-reported body type predicts the likelihood of explicitly signaling physical appearance preferences in a partner essay, with Athletic/Fit/Jacked respondents signaling at the highest rate (4.2\%) and Full/Overweight respondents at the lowest (2.7\%). The pattern is consistent with assortative mating theory: research on exercise frequency and mate value finds a significant positive correlation between individuals' self-perceptions of mate value and the mate value they seek in ideal partners, consistent with the "like-seeks-like" hypothesis (Stenstrom et al., 2020), suggesting that physically fit users who invest in their own fitness set correspondingly higher physical standards for a partner. This leads to the question of whether the signal reflects genuine preference or a strategic norm, since openly demanding physical attractiveness in a partner carries social costs that may suppress signaling among groups who face more stigma around appearance-based selection.
### Diet by Sex: 
```{r}
ggplot(df, aes(x = sex, fill = diet)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  labs(
    title = "Diet by Sex",
    subtitle = "Compares the diet composition within each sex group",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Proportion",
    fill = "Diet"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave(
  filename = "DietBySex.png",
  bg       = "white"
)
```
### Age by Drinking Behavior, Faceted by Sex: 
```{r}
ggplot(df, aes(x = drinks, y = age)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~ sex) +
  labs(
    title = "Age by Drinking Behavior, Faceted by Sex",
    subtitle = "Compares age distributions across drinking categories separately for each sex",
    x = "Drinks",
    y = "Age"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

ggsave(
  filename = "AgeByDrinkingBySex.png",
  bg       = "white"
)
```
### Age by Body Type: 
```{r}
ggplot(df, aes(x = body_type, y = age)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Age by Body Type",
    subtitle = "Shows variation in age across body type categories",
    x = "Body Type",
    y = "Age"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

ggsave(
  filename = "AgeByBodyType.png",
  bg       = "white"
)
```
# Exploration Log

While creating our visualizations, our main focus was on determining how to present the OkCupid dataset in a way that created a clear story and message. We first examined the univariate variables to see how the dataset was constructed and identify any particular variables of interest. The dataset could roughly be divided into 2 different types: standard demographic variables and essay responses. The essay responses played a key role in many of our bivariate visualizations. When extracting usable data from the essays, we selected a variety of keywords and then found the percentage of responses that contained at least one of the keywords.

For the visualizations, we placed a particular emphasis on age and preferences/demographic differences across age groups. For example, we looked at preferences for an educated partner across different age groups (with preference being determined by essay responses). We found that there was a positive correlation between age and desire for an educated partner. We repeated a similar process for other essay-based variables and their relations to age. This process allowed us to create a compelling story for how age correlates with matching preferences.

# Section C: Five Poster Figures.

See attached images
```{r}

```
# Section D – Variable Rationale

## Expert Sources
1. Castro, Á., & Barrada, J. R. (2020). Dating Apps and Their Sociodemographic and Psychosocial Correlates: A Systematic Review. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 17(18), 6500. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph17186500 

2. Dating App Revenue and Usage Statistics (2026). (n.d.). Business of Apps. Retrieved April 2, 2026, from https://www.businessofapps.com/data/dating-app-market/

3. Dating Statistics And Facts. (2025, July 22). Forbes Health. https://www.forbes.com/health/dating/dating-statistics/

4. Vogels, E. A., & McClain, C. (2023, February 2). Key findings about online dating in the U.S. Pew Research Center. https://www.pewresearch.org/short-reads/2023/02/02/key-findings-about-online-dating-in-the-u-s/

5. lResearch shows dating apps, sites are leading to more diverse couples. (n.d.). UNM UCAM Newsroom. Retrieved April 2, 2026, from https://news.unm.edu/news/research-shows-dating-apps-sites-are-leading-to-more-diverse-couplesimk

# Part E

Part E (Calais):
I’m most confident in our visualizations showing age, education assortative mating by age, and relationship goal signaling by age. These 3 visualizations all help to create a clear story about how the things people value in a relationship change	 over time. I’m less certain about the other 2 visualizations we have, especially the one about language use by age. This visualization speaks less about assortative mating and more about how younger demographics are more diverse than in the past. When it comes to feedback, I would most appreciate advice on how to combine these visualizations when discussing our primary observation that dating preferences change with age. 

Part E (Cat):
I am most confident in the poster figure that depicts assortative education levels based on different age groups. This is because it shows a clear pattern that as age increases, preference for higher educated individuals also increases. The figure I am less confident with is the language spoken by age group. There are no apparent differences between the age groups which doesn’t make the figure very appealing. One dead end that we reached was trying to find data on how dating apps are affecting individuals and their mental health. I learned how to modify our idea and still maintain the aspect about dating apps. The most useful feedback I would like to receive is whether the figures are able to tell a story and flow with one another. 

Part E (Juliene): 
I’m most confident in our bivariate visualizations, and I think they will be interesting and useful in analyzing trends and making a wider anaysis on society. I'm less certain about how we will be able to tie in our visualizations and analysis of trends to a strong societal argument. 
<!-- RNOTEBOOK_RMD_STATE  -->
